whatIsContributor.question: What is a contributor in Quantifier?
whatIsContributor.answer: A contributor is any team member who provides data, evidence, or confirmations for compliance tasks. You do not need compliance expertise — the platform guides you through each task with clear instructions.

howGetTasks.question: How do I receive tasks?
howGetTasks.answer: You receive notifications via email and in the platform when a compliance manager assigns you a task. Each task includes clear instructions, deadlines, and context about what is needed.

whatUpload.question: What kind of evidence can I upload?
whatUpload.answer: You can upload any file type — screenshots, PDFs, spreadsheets, documents, or images. The platform tells you exactly what evidence is needed for each task.

noCompliance.question: Do I need compliance knowledge?
noCompliance.answer: No. Tasks are designed to be completed by anyone. Forms include guidance, examples, and validation. If you have questions, you can comment directly on the task.

timeNeeded.question: How much time does it take?
timeNeeded.answer: Most contributor tasks take 5-15 minutes. The platform is designed to minimize the time non-compliance staff spend on compliance activities.

whatIsAuditorView.question: What can auditors access in Quantifier?
whatIsAuditorView.answer: Auditors get a dedicated read-only workspace with control-to-evidence mappings, document repositories, audit trails, and export capabilities for all compliance frameworks.

howAccess.question: How do auditors get access?
howAccess.answer: Administrators can create auditor accounts with read-only permissions. Auditors see only the controls, evidence, and trails relevant to their audit scope.

evidenceExport.question: Can I export evidence packages?
evidenceExport.answer: Yes. Export structured audit packages including all evidence files, approval records, audit trails, and compliance reports in ZIP or PDF format.

readOnly.question: Is auditor access read-only?
readOnly.answer: Yes. Auditor accounts have strict read-only permissions. They can view and export data but cannot modify any compliance records, evidence, or configurations.

multiFramework.question: Can I audit multiple frameworks at once?
multiFramework.answer: Yes. The auditor workspace supports multi-framework audits. You can review controls and evidence across ISO 27001, NIS2, DORA, GDPR, SOC 2, and more in one session.

cookies.metaDescription: Learn about how Quantifier.ai uses cookies and tracking technologies on our website.

whatAreCookies.title: What are cookies?
whatAreCookies.content: Cookies are small text files that are stored on your device when you visit websites. They help websites remember information about your visit, making your next visit easier and more useful.

cookiesWeUse.title: Cookies we use
cookiesWeUse.googleAnalytics: We use Google Analytics to understand how visitors interact with our website. This helps us improve user experience and measure the effectiveness of our content.

emailCollection.title: Email collection
emailCollection.content: We collect email addresses only when users voluntarily submit them through our forms, such as newsletter subscriptions or resource downloads. This data is stored securely and used only for the purposes described at the time of collection. You can unsubscribe from our communications at any time.

manageCookies.title: Managing cookies
manageCookies.content: You can control and delete cookies through your browser settings. Please note that disabling cookies may affect the functionality of our website. Most browsers allow you to refuse cookies or delete them after they have been set.

contact.title: Contact
contact.content: If you have any questions about our cookie policy, please contact us at: privacy@quantifier.ai

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.metaDescription: Learn how Quantifier.ai collects, uses, and protects your personal data.

admin.title: 1. Data Controller
admin.content: The controller of your personal data is Quantifier (the Quantifier.ai service). The Controller's identification details and registered address are available on the Website in the Contact section.
admin.contactEmail: For data protection enquiries, please contact:

dataProcessed.title: 2. Data We Process
dataProcessed.intro: Depending on how you use the Website, we may process:
dataProcessed.formData: data you provide through forms (e.g. your email address when subscribing to the newsletter, or data and message content when contacting us or requesting a demo);
dataProcessed.technicalData: technical and operational data related to your use of the Website (e.g. IP address, cookie identifiers, browser and device information, server logs).

purposes.title: 3. Purposes and Legal Bases for Processing

newsletter.title: 3.1 Newsletter and Email Marketing
newsletter.content: If you subscribe to our newsletter, we process your email address to send you the newsletter and commercial or marketing information about Quantifier (e.g. updates, offers, resources, events).
newsletter.electronicComm: E-Privacy requirements: marketing communications are sent only after obtaining your prior consent.
newsletter.withdrawal: You may withdraw your consent at any time by clicking the unsubscribe link in the email footer or by contacting us at contact@quantifier.ai. Withdrawal of consent does not affect the lawfulness of processing carried out prior to withdrawal.

contact.title: 3.2 Contact, Enquiries and Demo Requests
contact.content: When you contact us (e.g. request a demo), we process the data provided in your correspondence to handle the enquiry and communicate with you.
contact.legalBasis: Legal basis: taking steps at your request prior to entering into a contract, or performance of a contract (Article 6(1)(b) GDPR), where applicable, and the Controller's legitimate interest (Article 6(1)(f) GDPR) in handling enquiries and maintaining communication.

security.title: 3.3 Website Security and Operation
security.content: We process technical data (e.g. logs, IP addresses) to ensure security, detect abuse, and maintain and improve the Website.
security.legalBasis: Legal basis: the Controller's legitimate interest (Article 6(1)(f) GDPR).

recipients.title: 4. Data Recipients
recipients.intro: Your data may be shared with entities that support us in operating the Website and communications, including:
recipients.analytics: analytics and security tool providers (where used on the Website);
recipients.legal: entities entitled to receive data under applicable law.
recipients.processors: Data processors act on our behalf under data processing agreements and only to the extent necessary to deliver their services.

googleServices.title: 5. Google Services
googleServices.content: We may use Google services on the Website, including Google Analytics and Google reCAPTCHA. Use of these services is subject to Google's policies:
googleServices.recaptchaNote: Where reCAPTCHA is used, a notice about form protection by reCAPTCHA, together with the above links, may be displayed in the Website interface.

internationalTransfer.title: 6. Transfers Outside the EEA
internationalTransfer.content: Due to our use of service providers (e.g. analytics or security tools), your data may be transferred outside the European Economic Area. In such cases, we apply the transfer mechanisms required by law (e.g. Standard Contractual Clauses) or other legal bases permitted under applicable regulations. For details on how Google processes data, please refer to the Google policies linked above.

retention.title: 7. Data Retention Periods
retention.newsletter: Newsletter: until you withdraw consent or successfully unsubscribe; thereafter, for the period necessary to demonstrate consent was given or withdrawn, and for the defence of legal claims.
retention.contact: Contact / demo: for the duration of handling the matter and correspondence, then for the limitation period for claims or until you successfully object—depending on the legal basis.
retention.technical: Technical data (logs): for the period necessary to ensure security and diagnostics, and in accordance with our internal retention schedules.
retention.cookies: Cookies: in accordance with cookie lifespans and your settings (see the Cookies section for details).

rights.title: 8. Your Rights
rights.portability: data portability (where processing is based on consent or a contract and carried out by automated means);
rights.objection: object to processing based on legitimate interests;
rights.withdrawConsent: withdraw consent at any time (where processing is based on consent).
rights.howToExercise: To exercise your rights, please contact us:

complaint.title: 9. Right to Lodge a Complaint
complaint.content: If you believe we are processing your data unlawfully, you have the right to lodge a complaint with the relevant supervisory authority (in Poland: the President of the Personal Data Protection Office, UODO).

mandatory.title: 10. Is Providing Data Mandatory?
mandatory.newsletter: Newsletter: providing your email address is voluntary but necessary to receive the newsletter.
mandatory.contact: Contact / demo: providing data is voluntary but may be necessary to respond to your enquiry or fulfil your request.

automatedDecisions.title: 11. Automated Decision-Making
automatedDecisions.content: We do not make decisions about you based solely on automated processing, including profiling, that would produce legal effects concerning you or similarly significantly affect you.

cookies.title: 12. Cookies
cookies.content: The Website uses cookies and similar technologies. Essential cookies are used to ensure the proper functioning and security of the Website. Analytical and functional cookies (where used) may be deployed based on your consent, which you may withdraw or modify at any time via the cookie settings available on the Website (if we provide a consent panel) or your browser settings.
cookies.manage: You can manage cookies in your browser settings. Restricting cookies may affect the functionality of certain Website features.

changes.title: 13. Changes to this Privacy Policy
changes.content: We may update this Privacy Policy, particularly in the event of changes to the way the Website operates or to applicable laws. The current version is published on this page together with the date of the last update.

whatIsAiOfficer.question: What is an AI Compliance Officer?

howDifferent.question: How is this different from a compliance checklist tool?
howDifferent.answer: Unlike static checklist tools, Quantifier's AI agent understands context. It learns your business, tracks changes across multiple frameworks simultaneously, takes autonomous action (like assigning tasks and drafting documents), and adapts its recommendations based on your specific compliance posture.

whichFrameworks.question: Which compliance frameworks does the AI support?
whichFrameworks.answer: The AI Compliance Officer supports ISO 27001, NIS2, DORA, GDPR, SOC 2, ISO 9001, ISO 14001, CSRD/ESG, and more. It's continuously updated as standards evolve, and can handle multiple frameworks simultaneously with cross-mapping capabilities.

dataPrivacy.question: Is my compliance data secure?
dataPrivacy.answer: Yes. Quantifier is hosted on EU infrastructure, all data is encrypted at rest and in transit, and we follow strict data isolation practices. Your compliance data is never used to train AI models or shared with third parties.

setupTime.question: How long does it take to set up?
setupTime.answer: Most organizations are up and running within 1-2 weeks. The AI begins learning your compliance context immediately upon data import, and starts providing actionable insights within the first few days.

whatIsValueChain.question: What is value chain management in GRC?
whatIsValueChain.answer: Value chain management in GRC involves mapping, assessing, and monitoring all third-party suppliers and vendors for compliance risks. Quantifier automates supplier due diligence, risk scoring, and continuous monitoring across your entire supply chain.

howScoring.question: How does supplier risk scoring work?
howScoring.answer: Suppliers are scored based on their questionnaire responses, certifications held, audit results, and compliance posture. The scoring algorithm weighs factors relevant to your specific framework requirements and risk appetite.

whichFrameworks.question: Which frameworks require supply chain management?
whichFrameworks.answer: NIS2 (Article 21), ISO 27001 (Annex A.15), DORA (ICT third-party risk), CSRD (value chain reporting), and GDPR (data processor management) all have supply chain compliance requirements that Quantifier addresses.

supplierOnboarding.question: How do I onboard new suppliers?
supplierOnboarding.answer: Add suppliers to the platform, send automated assessment questionnaires, collect certifications and evidence, and receive a risk score. The entire onboarding process is tracked and documented for audit purposes.

continuousMonitoring.question: What does continuous monitoring include?
continuousMonitoring.answer: Continuous monitoring tracks certification expiry dates, compliance status changes, periodic reassessment schedules, and external risk signals. You receive automated alerts when supplier risk profiles change.

whatIsDms.question: What is compliance document management?
whatIsDms.answer: Compliance document management is the systematic organization, versioning, approval, and storage of all documents required for regulatory compliance. Quantifier provides a centralized repository designed specifically for GRC documentation needs.

versionControl.question: How does version control work?
versionControl.answer: Every document change is automatically versioned. You can view the complete change history, compare versions side by side, and rollback to any previous version if needed. All changes are logged in the audit trail.

whichFrameworks.question: Which framework documents can I manage?
whichFrameworks.answer: Quantifier supports document management for ISO 27001, NIS2, DORA, GDPR, SOC 2, ESG/CSRD, and more. Each framework comes with template structures and required document checklists.

accessControl.question: How are document permissions managed?
accessControl.answer: Documents use role-based access control (RBAC). You can set permissions at folder or document level, restrict editing to specific roles, and ensure sensitive documents are only visible to authorized personnel.

auditTrail.question: Is there a complete audit trail?
auditTrail.answer: Yes. Every action — view, edit, approve, download, share — is logged with timestamps and user identity. This audit trail is immutable and can be exported for external auditors.

howAssign.question: How do I assign tasks to team members?
howAssign.answer: You can assign tasks directly from the task board or list view. Set assignees, deadlines, priorities, and add context from the relevant framework requirement. Team members receive notifications and can track their tasks in a personal dashboard.

whichFrameworks.question: Which compliance frameworks are supported?

dataCollection.question: How does data collection work?

automation.question: Can I automate recurring tasks?
automation.answer: Yes. Set up recurring tasks for periodic reviews, audits, and assessments. Quantifier automatically creates new task instances, assigns them, and tracks completion against your compliance calendar.

whatIsRiskAssessment.question: What is compliance risk assessment?
whatIsRiskAssessment.answer: Compliance risk assessment is the systematic process of identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and treating risks that could affect your organization regulatory compliance. Quantifier provides a structured methodology aligned with ISO 31000 and framework-specific requirements.

methodology.question: What risk assessment methodology does Quantifier use?
methodology.answer: Quantifier supports both qualitative (impact-likelihood matrices) and quantitative risk assessment methods. You can customize scoring scales, risk categories, and treatment options to match your organization risk appetite and framework requirements.

whichFrameworks.question: Which frameworks require risk assessment?
whichFrameworks.answer: Virtually all compliance frameworks require risk assessment: ISO 27001 (Clause 6.1), NIS2 (Article 21), DORA (Chapter II), GDPR (Article 35 DPIA), SOC 2, and ISO 31000. Quantifier maps risks to specific framework controls automatically.

riskMatrix.question: How does the risk heatmap work?
riskMatrix.answer: The risk heatmap visualizes all identified risks on an impact-likelihood matrix. Risks are color-coded by severity level (critical, high, medium, low), making it easy to prioritize treatment efforts and communicate risk posture to stakeholders.

automation.question: Can risk assessments be automated?
automation.answer: Yes. Quantifier automates periodic risk reassessments, inherent/residual risk calculation, risk score updates based on treatment progress, and alert generation when risk levels change. You can also set up automated risk reporting schedules.

items.question: What ESG frameworks does Quantifier support?
items.answer: Quantifier covers CSRD/ESRS, GRI Standards, and CBAM reporting. The platform maps disclosure requirements, tracks data collection progress, and generates audit-ready reports for each framework.

items.question: How does the Envirly integration work?
items.answer: Envirly — part of the Quantifier ecosystem — specializes in environmental compliance: GHG accounting (Scope 1-3), LCA analysis, carbon footprint calculations, and CSRD reporting. Data flows seamlessly between both platforms.

items.question: Can Quantifier handle double materiality assessments?
items.answer: Yes. The platform guides you through the double materiality assessment process required by CSRD, helping identify material topics from both impact and financial perspectives with stakeholder input tracking.

items.question: Is CBAM reporting automated?
items.answer: Quantifier automates CBAM data collection from importers, calculates embedded emissions per product category, and generates quarterly CBAM reports in the format required by EU authorities.

items.question: How do you ensure ESG data quality?
items.answer: The platform includes built-in validation rules, audit trails for every data point, and AI-powered anomaly detection. All calculations follow recognized methodologies (GHG Protocol, ISO 14064).

items.question: What environmental standards does Quantifier support?
items.answer: Quantifier covers ISO 14001 (Environmental Management), Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), Carbon Footprint calculations (Scope 1-3), and Decarbonisation pathway planning — all within a single platform.

items.question: Can I calculate my organization carbon footprint?
items.answer: Yes. The Carbon Footprint module follows the GHG Protocol methodology, covering Scope 1 (direct emissions), Scope 2 (energy), and Scope 3 (value chain). Data collection is automated with supplier integration.

items.question: How does ISO 14001 compliance work in Quantifier?
items.answer: The platform maps all ISO 14001 clauses to actionable tasks, tracks environmental aspects and impacts, manages documentation, and prepares you for certification and surveillance audits.

items.question: What is Life Cycle Assessment and do I need it?
items.answer: LCA evaluates the environmental impact of a product across its entire lifecycle. It is increasingly required by regulations (CSRD, EU Taxonomy) and valuable for product design decisions and green marketing claims.

items.question: Can I set and track decarbonisation targets?
items.answer: Yes. The Decarbonisation module helps you set science-based targets (SBTi-aligned), create reduction roadmaps, track progress against baselines, and report on climate commitments.

items.question: What governance areas does Quantifier cover?

items.question: Is the whistleblowing channel compliant with EU regulations?

items.question: How does policy management work?
items.answer: Quantifier automates the full policy lifecycle: drafting (AI-assisted), review and approval workflows, version control, employee distribution with read-receipt tracking, and periodic review reminders.

items.question: Can employees report anonymously?

items.question: How does this integrate with other compliance frameworks?
items.answer: Governance policies and whistleblowing procedures are cross-referenced with framework requirements (NIS2, DORA, ISO 27001). When a policy is updated, related compliance evidence is automatically refreshed.

items.question: What is a Digital Product Passport (DPP)?
items.answer: A DPP is a digital record containing information about a product materials, manufacturing, certifications, and end-of-life handling. The EU Battery Regulation and Ecodesign Regulation require DPPs for certain product categories starting 2027.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with DPP compliance?
items.answer: Quantifier automates data collection across your supply chain, generates standardized DPPs, manages QR code linking, and ensures your product data meets evolving EU requirements.

items.question: What is the difference between DPP and LCA?
items.answer: DPP is a product-level data record required by regulation. LCA (Life Cycle Assessment) is an analytical methodology measuring environmental impact across a product lifecycle. Quantifier supports both — LCA data can feed directly into your DPP.

items.question: Which industries need Digital Product Passports?
items.answer: Starting with batteries (2027), the DPP requirement will expand to textiles, construction materials, electronics, and other sectors under the EU Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR).

items.question: Can I manage product compliance across multiple regions?
items.answer: Yes. Quantifier supports multi-region compliance requirements, tracking which products need DPPs in which markets, and adapting documentation to local regulatory requirements.

nis2Directive.definition: The NIS2 Directive (Network and Information Security Directive 2) is a European Union cybersecurity regulation that establishes minimum security requirements for network and information systems across essential and important sectors. It replaces the original NIS Directive and significantly expands its scope.

essentialEntities.definition: Organizations in critical sectors (energy, transport, health, banking, digital infrastructure, public administration) with more than 250 employees or annual turnover exceeding EUR 50 million. Essential entities face stricter requirements, including 24-hour incident reporting and potential personal liability for management.

importantEntities.definition: Medium-sized organizations (50-250 employees) operating in sectors covered by NIS2 but not classified as essential. Important entities must comply with the same security requirements but face less stringent supervision and lower maximum penalties.

incidentReporting.definition: Under NIS2, significant cybersecurity incidents must be reported to national authorities within 24 hours of detection (early warning), followed by a detailed report within 72 hours and a final report within one month. Non-compliance can result in fines up to EUR 10 million or 2% of global turnover.

items.question: What is SOC 2?
items.answer: SOC 2 is an auditing framework developed by the American Institute of CPAs (AICPA) that assesses service organizations' controls related to security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy.

items.question: Who needs SOC 2 certification?
items.answer: Any organization that handles customer data, particularly SaaS companies, cloud service providers, data centers, and technology vendors selling to enterprise clients.

items.question: How long does SOC 2 certification take?
items.answer: With proper preparation and automation tools like Quantifier, organizations can achieve SOC 2 certification in 3-6 months. Without automation, it typically takes 6-12 months.

items.question: What are the 5 Trust Services Criteria?
items.answer: The Trust Services Criteria are: Security (required), Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy. Security is mandatory; others are selected based on your service offerings.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with SOC 2?
items.answer: Quantifier automates evidence collection from your systems, continuously monitors controls, identifies gaps, and keeps you audit-ready with real-time dashboards and reporting.

items.question: Is SOC 2 mandatory?
items.answer: While not legally required, SOC 2 is often a contractual requirement for B2B SaaS companies. Over 80% of enterprise buyers require SOC 2 certification before signing vendor contracts.

items.question: What is ISO 27001?

items.question: Who needs ISO 27001 certification?

items.question: What are the costs involved?
items.answer: Costs include platform/consultant fees, staff time, and certification body fees. Quantifier helps reduce total cost of ownership by automating manual tasks and reducing the need for expensive consultants.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with certification?

items.question: Is ISO 27001 mandatory?
items.answer: ISO 27001 is voluntary, but increasingly required by enterprise customers, regulators, and partners. Many contracts now mandate certification, and it helps demonstrate GDPR, HIPAA, and other regulatory compliance.

items.question: What is DORA?
items.answer: DORA (Digital Operational Resilience Act) is an EU regulation that establishes uniform requirements for the security of network and information systems of companies and organizations operating in the financial sector. It aims to ensure financial entities can withstand, respond to, and recover from ICT-related disruptions.

items.question: Who does DORA apply to?
items.answer: DORA applies to a wide range of financial entities including banks, insurance companies, investment firms, payment institutions, crypto-asset service providers, and ICT third-party service providers that serve the financial sector.

items.question: When did DORA come into effect?
items.answer: DORA entered into force on January 16, 2023, with a 24-month implementation period. Full enforcement began on January 17, 2025, meaning all in-scope entities must now comply with DORA requirements.

items.question: What are the penalties for DORA non-compliance?
items.answer: Penalties for DORA non-compliance can be significant, including fines of up to 10 million euros or 5% of annual turnover for entities, and up to 1 million euros for individuals. Regulators can also impose operational restrictions.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with DORA compliance?
items.answer: Quantifier automates key DORA requirements including ICT risk management, incident reporting, third-party risk oversight, and resilience testing. Our AI-powered platform provides continuous monitoring, automated evidence collection, and streamlined regulatory reporting.

items.question: What are the 5 pillars of DORA?
items.answer: The 5 pillars of DORA are: ICT Risk Management, ICT-Related Incident Reporting, Digital Operational Resilience Testing, ICT Third-Party Risk Management, and Information Sharing Arrangements.

fashionRetailer.title: ESG Compliance Journey of a Fashion Retailer
fashionRetailer.section1Text: This well-established European fashion retailer has long championed quality and design longevity as part of its brand identity. These values inherently align with the principles of sustainable development. As the regulatory environment around sustainability disclosures evolved, the company recognized the need to transition from informal practices to structured ESG compliance, particularly in light of the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).
fashionRetailer.section2Intro: Despite a history of responsible product design and resource use, it was the upcoming CSRD requirements that catalyzed the company's formal ESG journey. The leadership team viewed ESG reporting as both a regulatory obligation and a strategic opportunity to:
fashionRetailer.section2Item1: Strengthen customer engagement by showcasing responsible business practices
fashionRetailer.section2Item2: Prepare the organization for investor and stakeholder expectations
fashionRetailer.section2Outro: The company also identified a growing commercial upside in ESG efforts. Consumer willingness to pay a premium for sustainable products and packaging reinforced the business case for transparent sustainability reporting.
fashionRetailer.section3Intro: To navigate the compliance process efficiently and accurately, the company partnered with Envirly, a platform combining advanced ESG software with expert advisory.
fashionRetailer.section3Item1: In-depth regulatory knowledge (CSRD, ESRS, GHG Protocol)
fashionRetailer.section3Item3: High user satisfaction with the platform's intuitive interface
fashionRetailer.section4Intro: To ensure smooth implementation and compliance, the company created a steering committee, composed of:
fashionRetailer.section4Item3: A representative from finance and administration
fashionRetailer.section4Outro: This core team was supported by stakeholders from HR, logistics, IT, controlling, and product development. The company also plans to bring on a dedicated ESG coordinator to oversee long-term sustainability strategy and regulatory reporting.
fashionRetailer.section5Title: 05. Outcomes of the ESG Compliance Process
fashionRetailer.section5Item1: Full visibility into emissions across Scopes 1, 2, and 3
fashionRetailer.section5Item2: Streamlined data collection and internal ESG processes
fashionRetailer.section5Item3: Enhanced stakeholder alignment around sustainability goals
fashionRetailer.section5Item4: Organizational readiness for upcoming ESRS-aligned reporting obligations
fashionRetailer.summaryItem1Text: CSRD compliance requires months of preparation and organizational coordination.
fashionRetailer.summaryItem2Text: A reliable partner helps demystify complex regulations.
fashionRetailer.summaryItem3Text: ESG is a horizontal topic that spans departments.
fashionRetailer.summaryItem4Text: Assign ownership to ensure the process stays on track.

logisticsGroup.title: A Leading Logistics Group's ESG Transformation
logisticsGroup.section1Text: The company has been actively engaged in sustainability initiatives for many years, including supporting businesses in over 150 countries in developing modern workplace cultures built on trust and performance.
logisticsGroup.section2Text: The biggest challenge was identifying and collecting the right data for ESG reporting—particularly carbon footprint data, which is critical in the TSL sector. As a logistics operator, not just a transport provider, the company required expert guidance to determine which data sets were necessary to meet the requirements of the CSRD directive.
logisticsGroup.section3Intro: The collaboration consisted of several key phases:
logisticsGroup.section3Item1: Double Materiality Assessment – identifying the most relevant environmental, social, and governance topics for the company and its stakeholders
logisticsGroup.section3Item2: Carbon Footprint Calculation – estimation of GHG emissions across Scopes 1, 2, and 3
logisticsGroup.section3Item3: Governance & HR Policy Analysis – including anti-mobbing procedures, whistleblower protection, and anti-corruption measures
logisticsGroup.section3Item4: Sustainability Strategy Development – building internal know-how and capabilities
logisticsGroup.section3Item5: Training for Employees – to ensure a broader understanding of ESG principles
logisticsGroup.section3Item6: ESG Communication Strategy – defining the company's narrative and messaging
logisticsGroup.section4Title: 05. "We Want to Stay Ahead of Market Demands"
logisticsGroup.section4Text: The client values competitiveness across all aspects of its business. It continuously improves TSL processes, measures performance, and assesses the effectiveness of initiatives across all subsidiaries. Through the partnership with Envirly, the organization gained tools and skills for monitoring, analyzing, and verifying sustainability metrics and ESG data.
logisticsGroup.summaryItem1: Comprehensive ESG strategy aligned with industry best practices
logisticsGroup.summaryItem2: Accurate carbon footprint measurement across all operations
logisticsGroup.summaryItem3: Enhanced internal capabilities for ongoing ESG management
logisticsGroup.summaryItem4: Competitive advantage through early CSRD compliance

financialServices.title: Driving ESG Compliance Through Innovation in Financial Services
financialServices.section1Text: As ESG regulations grow more demanding, especially with the upcoming Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), the bank recognized the urgent need to support its clients—especially SMEs and corporate clients—in aligning with these new standards.
financialServices.section2Text: "Companies need more than capital—they need support in meeting ESG goals and compliance requirements," said the bank's Executive Director of Sustainable Development. The chosen solution—a digital ESG compliance platform—supports reporting aligned with GHG Protocol and ISO 14064-1. During the pilot phase, clients used the platform to calculate their emissions and track reductions linked to energy efficiency improvements and building upgrades.
financialServices.section3Intro: Through the partnership, the bank equipped its clients with tools to:
financialServices.section3Item3: Visualize ESG data with dashboards, infographics, and automated reports
financialServices.section3Item4: Identify hotspots and track progress toward climate goals
financialServices.section3Outro: The solution, powered by artificial intelligence, also simplifies the complex process of ESG data aggregation and supply chain emissions tracking—a critical capability for upcoming CSRD-aligned disclosures.
financialServices.section4Benefit1Text: Business clients gained access to the platform at no cost during the pilot and now benefit from tailored ESG support at preferential rates.
financialServices.section4Benefit2Text: The solution provides structured, regulation-aligned reporting, helping clients prepare for CSRD requirements and beyond.
financialServices.section4Benefit3Text: Clients identified high-emission activities and implemented reduction strategies, leading to greater operational efficiency and lower costs.
financialServices.section4Benefit4Text: The platform delivers accurate, verifiable emissions data based on robust methodologies, supporting informed and credible decision-making.
financialServices.section5Intro: Thanks to this collaboration, the bank's clients can now:
financialServices.section5Item2: Comply with CSRD and other ESG regulations
financialServices.section5Outro: The platform's automation and AI capabilities reduce the burden of ESG reporting while enhancing transparency and stakeholder trust.
financialServices.section6Text1: This case study highlights how strategic partnerships between financial institutions and ESG tech providers can accelerate compliance, empower businesses, and contribute to the broader EU goal of net-zero emissions by 2050.
financialServices.section6Text2: By digitizing ESG data management and making compliance tools broadly available, the bank plays a critical role in building market resilience, climate awareness, and long-term competitiveness among its clients.

items.question: What is GDPR?
items.answer: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a comprehensive EU data protection law that gives individuals control over their personal data. It applies to any organization processing personal data of EU residents, regardless of where the organization is based.

items.question: Who does GDPR apply to?
items.answer: GDPR applies to any organization that collects, processes, or stores personal data of EU residents. This includes companies headquartered outside the EU if they offer goods or services to EU citizens or monitor their behavior.

items.question: What are the penalties for GDPR non-compliance?
items.answer: GDPR violations can result in fines of up to €20 million or 4% of annual global turnover, whichever is higher. Additionally, organizations may face reputational damage, legal action from data subjects, and regulatory restrictions.

items.question: What are data subject rights under GDPR?
items.answer: GDPR grants individuals eight key rights: the right to be informed, right of access, right to rectification, right to erasure, right to restrict processing, right to data portability, right to object, and rights related to automated decision-making and profiling.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with GDPR compliance?
items.answer: Quantifier automates key GDPR requirements including data mapping, consent management, DSAR processing, DPIA workflows, breach notification, and continuous compliance monitoring. Our AI-powered platform reduces manual work by 80% and ensures you meet all 99 GDPR articles.

items.question: Does GDPR apply to companies outside the EU?
items.answer: Yes, GDPR has extraterritorial scope. Any company worldwide that offers goods or services to EU residents or monitors their behavior must comply with GDPR, regardless of where the company is located.

items.question: What is HIPAA?
items.answer: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) is a US federal law enacted in 1996 that establishes national standards for protecting sensitive patient health information. It applies to covered entities and their business associates who handle Protected Health Information (PHI).

items.question: Who must comply with HIPAA?
items.answer: HIPAA applies to covered entities including healthcare providers, health plans, and healthcare clearinghouses. It also applies to business associates—organizations that handle PHI on behalf of covered entities, such as IT vendors, billing companies, and cloud service providers.

items.question: What are the penalties for HIPAA violations?
items.answer: HIPAA violations can result in civil penalties ranging from $100 to $50,000 per violation, with annual maximums of $1.5 million per violation category. Criminal violations can result in fines up to $250,000 and imprisonment up to 10 years for offenses committed with intent to sell PHI.

items.question: What is Protected Health Information (PHI)?
items.answer: PHI includes any individually identifiable health information that is transmitted or maintained in any form. This includes patient names, addresses, dates, Social Security numbers, medical records, billing information, and any data that could identify a patient.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with HIPAA compliance?
items.answer: Quantifier automates key HIPAA requirements including PHI discovery and protection, access control management, audit trail maintenance, breach detection and notification, staff training management, and continuous compliance monitoring. Our AI-powered platform reduces manual compliance work by 75%.

items.question: What is the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule?
items.answer: The Breach Notification Rule requires covered entities to notify affected individuals within 60 days of discovering a breach affecting more than 500 individuals. Smaller breaches must be reported annually to HHS. Quantifier automates breach detection and notification workflows to ensure timely compliance.

items.question: What is CCPA?
items.answer: The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) is a state privacy law that gives California residents the right to know what personal information businesses collect, request deletion of their data, opt-out of the sale of their personal information, and receive equal service regardless of their privacy choices.

items.question: Who must comply with CCPA?
items.answer: CCPA applies to for-profit businesses that collect California residents' personal information and meet at least one threshold: annual gross revenue over $25 million, buy/sell/share data of 100,000+ consumers or devices, or derive 50%+ of annual revenue from selling personal information.

items.question: What are the penalties for CCPA violations?
items.answer: CCPA violations can result in civil penalties of $2,500 per unintentional violation and $7,500 per intentional violation. Additionally, consumers have a private right of action for data breaches, with statutory damages of $100-$750 per consumer per incident, or actual damages if greater.

items.question: What consumer rights does CCPA provide?
items.answer: CCPA provides four main rights: the right to know what personal information is collected and how it's used, the right to delete personal information, the right to opt-out of the sale of personal information, and the right to non-discrimination for exercising privacy rights.

items.question: How does Quantifier help with CCPA compliance?

Our AI-powered platform reduces manual work by 70%.

items.question: How does CCPA differ from GDPR?
items.answer: While both protect consumer privacy, CCPA focuses specifically on California residents and emphasizes the right to opt-out of data sales. GDPR requires opt-in consent, applies to EU residents, and has broader scope. Quantifier helps organizations comply with both regulations from a single platform.

eventsHub.faq2q: Is ISO 27001 certification mandatory under NIS2?
eventsHub.faq2a: NIS2 does not mandate ISO 27001 certification, but in practice this standard is one of the most recognised frameworks for demonstrating the maturity of an information security management system. In many industries it is becoming a market standard.
eventsHub.faq3a: Yes. NIS2 explicitly assigns liability to management bodies for overseeing the cybersecurity system. This includes approving security measures, monitoring their effectiveness and ensuring adequate resources.
eventsHub.faq4q: How long does it realistically take to prepare an organisation?
eventsHub.faq4a: It depends on the level of maturity. Organisations with an established risk management framework can implement the requirements within a few months. Without governance structures, the process may be more complex and require a phased approach.
eventsHub.faq5a: Cybersecurity under NIS2 is not an IT project. The process should involve the board, IT, compliance, risk management, HR (training & awareness), legal and operations. In many cases procurement and supplier management are also essential.
eventsHub.faq6q: What are the consequences of not being prepared?
eventsHub.faq6a: Consequences include the risk of administrative sanctions, personal liability for board members, loss of contracts, increased cyber insurance costs and reputational damage.
eventsHub.faq7q: What is the difference between an 'implementation project' and a systemic approach?
eventsHub.faq7a: An implementation project ends once the documents are prepared. A systemic approach means continuous risk monitoring, updating evidence of compliance, managing incidents and operational reporting in a continuous compliance model.
eventsHub.faq8q: What does the incident reporting obligation look like?
eventsHub.faq8a: NIS2 introduces specific reporting deadlines and requires rapid submission of initial incident notifications. An organisation must have procedures for detecting, classifying, escalating and formally reporting events to the competent authority.
eventsHub.faq9a: In many cases, the key factor is not new tools but the integration of existing systems, organising assets, risk registers and evidence of compliance. Technology supports the process, but governance is the foundation.
eventsHub.faq10q: Is this series suitable for organisations just starting their preparations?
eventsHub.faq10a: Yes. The sessions are designed to provide boards with a clear action roadmap regardless of their current level of readiness. Participants receive a decision-making framework that helps determine the next step.

seo.titleEn: NIS2 Compliance Software — AI-Powered Risk & Evidence Platform | Quantifier.ai
seo.descEn: Automate NIS2 compliance with AI-native software. Gap analysis, risk management, incident reporting, supply chain security and continuous audit readiness in one platform.
seo.faq1a: NIS2 (EU 2022/2555) is the EU directive on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity. It expands requirements to essential and important entities across 18 sectors, covering risk management, incident reporting, supply chain security, and management accountability.
seo.faq2q: What are the penalties for NIS2 non-compliance?
seo.faq2a: Fines up to EUR 10 million or 2% of global annual turnover for essential entities, and EUR 7 million or 1.4% for important entities. Management can also be held personally liable.
seo.faq3q: Does NIS2 apply the same way in every EU country?
seo.faq3a: No. NIS2 is an EU directive that sets a baseline, but each member state transposes it into national law. Scope, deadlines, and enforcement details may differ between countries.
seo.faq4q: How long does NIS2 implementation take?
seo.faq4a: Typical implementation takes 6-18 months depending on current maturity. With compliance automation software, this can be reduced to 3-6 months.
seo.faq5q: Is ISO 27001 sufficient for NIS2 compliance?
seo.faq5a: ISO 27001 covers many NIS2 requirements but key gaps remain: incident reporting timelines (24h/72h), supply chain obligations, and management accountability provisions specific to NIS2.
seo.faq6a: Article 21 defines 10 categories of cybersecurity risk-management measures that essential and important entities must implement, including risk analysis, incident handling, business continuity, supply chain security, and cryptography.

items.answer: Sprinto's trust center publicly indicates hosting on AWS us-west-1 (California, USA). Organisations with EU data residency requirements should evaluate this against their compliance needs.

items.question: Does Quantifier support ESG and CSRD reporting?
items.answer: Yes. Quantifier natively supports CSRD, ESRS, carbon footprint, and EU Taxonomy reporting workflows — a clear differentiator over Sprinto.

items.question: Which platform is better for fast-growing startups?
items.answer: Sprinto is well-suited for startups focused on SOC 2 certification. Quantifier is better for organisations that also need EU regulatory compliance, ESG/CSRD, and value-chain governance.

items.question: How do AI capabilities compare?
items.answer: Both platforms offer AI-driven compliance workflows. Sprinto communicates autonomous trust and AI agents. Quantifier's AI Compliance Officer coordinates across EU regulatory frameworks with cross-framework evidence generation.

sprintoAlternative.disclaimer: Comparison based on publicly available product information as of March 2026. Feature availability may vary by plan, packaging, region, and implementation scope.
